headline: Designing
for
human
behnchod
hero.subtitle: HCI designer working across product, XR and physical interfaces — currently MSc HCI at the University of the Creative Arts, London.
hero.currently: Researching tangible & spatial UI · Open to product / HCI roles in London

projects.title: Metalane — Health Platform
projects.blurb: End-to-end digital health platform (now part of Illumina) — service discovery, appointments, family records and insurance, designed around accessibility and health literacy.

projects.title: Wild Wisdom — Education Platform
projects.blurb: An educational platform for young learners exploring wildlife and nature — playful flows, intuitive navigation, and motion sequences that bring content to life.

projects.title: WWF — Earth Hour Campaign
projects.blurb: Campaign visuals, posters and digital assets for WWF India’s Earth Hour — communicating an urgent environmental message within global WWF identity.

projects.title: Cheesecake Studios
projects.blurb: Founded a boutique studio in J&K — animated invitations, identities and short-form video in Dogri. Grew an organic community around regional design.

projects.title: HCI Lab — Portfolio
projects.blurb: Six critical reflections from FGCT7023 — wicked problems, communities of practice, participatory design, and the four orders of design, written across a semester of live research at UCA.

sections.label: Design Challenge I
sections.body: A deceptively generous premise: £300,000, twelve months, a campus — design the lab. Working solo surfaced instincts I hadn’t examined before: XR headsets across the price spectrum, motion capture, haptic wearables, a BCI/EEG setup. The proposal landed well. That’s precisely why I wanted to interrogate it.

sections.label: Wicked Problems
sections.body: Delhi’s winter smog maps onto all ten of Rittel and Webber’s properties. A 2025 AQI peak of 461 is not a design problem — it is a political, infrastructural, and generational condition that resists every single-actor intervention. HCI’s increasing role in civic systems means inheriting the wickedness.

sections.label: Communities of Practice
sections.body: Four or five of us interested in physical product design, none with a polished roadmap. We joined because the module defaults to screen-based work and we wanted to push toward objects you can hold. The group mapped cleanly onto Wenger-Trayner’s three CoP characteristics — we just didn’t know it at the time.

sections.label: Participatory Design
sections.body: Bødker, Kensing, and Simonsen’s starting position: not “users are a useful input” but “users are co-designers, and the political question is who gets to make the decisions.” Involving people in design is not the same as giving them authority over it. That distinction is the discipline.

sections.label: Buchanan’s Four Orders
sections.body: Graphics, products, interactions, systems. Working through the framework against this semester’s work revealed a consistent drift toward the third and fourth orders — a preference for actions and conditions over artefacts. The most honest thing the framework did was name what I had been avoiding: the question of scale.

sections.label: Requirements Engineering
sections.body: Sommerville’s four-stage process assumes requirements can be elicited, classified, and signed off. Working through the library-app exercise with real stakeholder tensions — students, staff, management — showed exactly where the procedure stops being able to help. The gap between method and reality is where the design actually lives.

projects.title: State of the Art — Portfolio
projects.blurb: Six field reports from FGCT7022 — AI in law, invisible interfaces, behavioural systems, XR embodiment, and a technology hunt at AWS Summit London, written from the edges of HCI’s expanding territory.

sections.label: AWS Summit Hunt
sections.body: A full day at AWS Summit London on a self-directed technology hunt. The standout: the Natural History Museum’s connected data ecosystem — urban sensors, community-science surveys, AI unlocking century-old specimens. Around a hundred new connections, and a much clearer sense of where these technologies are actually heading versus where the marketing claims they are.

sections.label: AI in Law
sections.body: Sharing a cab to work, a practising lawyer told me Harvey had done roughly 85% of the drafting on a recent financial legal document — and she now needed just twenty minutes of review before signing. That single detail made AI’s reach concrete rather than abstract. No field left untouched, including one as tradition-bound as law.

sections.label: Behavioural Systems
sections.body: We constructed a scale model of a public waiting room — referencing Waterloo Station — and systematically altered its spatial cues. The cardboard model became a test bed for one argument: designed environments are not neutral containers. The Gruen transfer is not an accident; it is the design.

sections.label: AI Trust & Control
sections.body: Autonomous systems optimising power grids, self-driving vehicles, co-working AI agents — all raise the same structural question: at what point does a system’s capability exceed a human’s ability to meaningfully oversee it? A speculative inquiry into where trust begins to substitute for understanding.

sections.label: XR & Embodied Interaction
sections.body: The question the XR workshops kept returning to: what unique actions can you do here that you cannot in other mediums? Beat Saber, Superhot VR, Half-Life: Alyx — each a demonstration that when the body becomes the interface, an entirely different set of design constraints applies.

sections.label: Invisible Interfaces
sections.body: Mark Weiser’s 1991 claim: the most profound technologies are those that disappear, weaving themselves into everyday life until they are indistinguishable from it. Before smartphones and GUIs, humans navigated a world dense with dials, textures, and learned conventions. Designing for invisibility is a distinct and demanding discipline.

xrProjects.title: Spatial Memory
xrProjects.tagline: A room that remembers where you left your attention.
xrProjects.body: A passthrough MR study exploring spatially-anchored notes and ambient recall — your kitchen counter becomes a calendar; your bookshelf, an inbox.

xrProjects.title: Tangible Dial
xrProjects.tagline: A knob that knows when you mean it.
xrProjects.body: A haptic rotary controller paired with a screen-based meditation app — tactile detents map to breath rhythm, not menu steps.

xrProjects.title: Ray-Ban · Glance Layer
xrProjects.body: A sound-led notification grammar for camera-glasses — micro-cues delivered through bone conduction, designed for the periphery of attention.

xrProjects.title: Paper Desk
xrProjects.tagline: Pen-and-paper as the source of truth.
xrProjects.body: A camera-tracked desk surface that reads paper sketches in real time, turning ink strokes into live UI prototypes for ideation sessions.

xrProjects.title: Field Radio
xrProjects.tagline: A small box for slow internet.
xrProjects.body: A handheld FM-style player tuned to RSS feeds and podcast streams. One dial. One button. No notifications, ever.

notes.text: People trust physical knobs more than invisible gestures.
Waiting screens become emotional spaces. Most are wasted.
Most interfaces ignore rhythm. They tick, they don’t breathe.
A confirmation dialog is a small theatre. Treat it like one.
Sound shapes trust faster than copy does.
Latency, when honest, can be beautiful. When hidden, it is dishonest.

about.paragraph1: I work across product design, AR/VR and motion — drawn to the spaces where digital interfaces meet physical behaviour. Currently completing an MSc HCI at UCA London, with a focus on interaction design, accessibility and user-centred research.
about.paragraph2: Before London, I designed an end-to-end health platform at Metalane (now Illumina), led visual storytelling for WWF India, and founded a boutique studio producing animated identities and short-form video in my hometown.

skills.Practice: HCI · Interaction Design · Product Design · AR / VR · Motion
skills.Tools: Figma · Adobe Suite · After Effects · Premiere · Code
skills.Methods: User Research · Prototyping · Usability Testing · Accessibility (WCAG)

contact.footnote: Based in London. Currently completing my MSc HCI at UCA and open to product / HCI roles where research, craft and usability intersect.
